event_blocks.summary: The final heat of the 200 Medley Relay commenced with a lively, theatrical entrance sequence as teams emerged through branded tunnels amidst crowd noise and background music. The Florida State team, in lane one, was followed by Indiana's famed 'candy stripe' team, featuring Bar, Benzing, Brooks, and King. Texas entered in white tracksuits, with commentators highlighting Olympian Chris Giuliano. Tennessee, in lane four, featured Ghi Caribe and Jordan Crooks. Florida's team, with Gator logos, made a bold entrance, with commentators noting Josh Liendo's inclusion. Arizona State's team, in white tracksuits, raised their arms in unison. The scene was further enlivened by event staff, ESPN camera crews, and seated officials. Arizona State's team, including Vergnes, Dobrzanski, Kulw, and Ilya Kharun on the third leg, emerged from a smoke-filled tunnel. The California Team, with Seeliger and Jack Alexy on anchor, walked past a large banner, noted to have three returning swimmers. NC State, in white tracksuits with red accents, entered with focused composure in lane eight. Swimmers then prepared on starting blocks within the '2025 NCAA SWIMMING & DIVING' adorned aquatic center, with event officials and ESPN camera crews present. Commentators discussed the third leg, mentioning Hubert Kosh, Kabe, and Josh Liendo, and anticipating an 'SEC matchup' involving Tennessee and Jordan Crooks.

Texas Team.description: Clad in white tracksuits with black accents; one swimmer raises a blue water bottle in celebration as they walk out, with commentators naming their swimmers as Maudlin, German, Present, Hubert Kosh, and Olympian Chris Giuliano (a transfer from Notre Dame).

Florida Team.description: In black hoodies with Gator logos and swim caps; they make hand gestures and head movements as they emerge, with commentators highlighting the significant presence of Josh Liendo, who was not on their SEC relay.

Event Officials.description: Seated along the poolside in light-colored shirts, observing the teams as they enter and prepare for the race.

Arizona State Team.description: Dressed in white tracksuits with hoodies, they emerge energetically from a smoke-filled tunnel, with commentators highlighting swimmer Ilya Kharun's presence on the third leg.

ESPN Camera Crew.description: Visible in black shirts with 'ESPN' logos, operating cameras and capturing the team entrances and poolside preparations.

Tennessee Team.description: Wearing black jackets and swim trunks; one holds a white towel, and they walk with a composed, focused demeanor, announced by commentators to be in lane four with Ghi Caribe on the third leg and Jordan Crooks on the fourth.

Indiana Team.description: Dressed in black hoodies and red-and-white striped pants; they pump fists and display energetic, confident body language, identified by commentators as 'the famed candy stripe' team, with swimmers Bar, Benzing, Brooks, and King mentioned.

Florida State Team.description: Wearing black swimwear and hoodies, with swim caps and goggles; they walk out confidently, some with hands raised or gesturing, and are announced by commentators as being in lane one for the 200 Medley Relay.

Branded Tunnels.description: Large, illuminated entryways with team names, logos, and swimmer names displayed prominently, emitting smoke for dramatic effect.

NC State Team.description: Clad in white tracksuits with red accents and swim caps, they display focused composure as they exit their tunnel, with commentators confirming their position in lane eight.

California Team.description: Wearing black hoodies and swim trunks, they walk confidently past their banner, with commentators noting they will be in lane seven and Jack Alexy on the anchor leg, mentioning three returning swimmers from last year's fourth-place finish.

Swimmers on Starting Blocks.description: Shirtless male swimmers in swim caps and goggles, standing on numbered starting blocks, adjusting gear and stretching in preparation for the race.

raw_segments: The segment opens with commentators setting the stage for the "last heat, timed final of the 200 Medley Relay," discussing "two different times and records" over a backdrop of lively crowd noise and background music. The dynamic and celebratory entrance sequence for the Men's 200 Yard Medley Relay at an NCAA swimming event begins as teams emerge one by one through branded tunnels, accompanied by dramatic smoke effects and team-specific visuals. First, the Florida State team, wearing black swimwear and hoodies, confidently steps out, with commentators announcing they will be in lane one. Next, Indiana’s team, in black hoodies and red-and-white striped pants, pumps their fists and gestures energetically, identified by commentators as "the Indiana swim team, the famed candy stripe," with swimmers Bar, Benzing, Brooks, and King highlighted. Texas follows in white tracksuits, one swimmer raising a blue water bottle, as commentators introduce their "brilliant swimmers" Maudlin, German, Present, Hubert Kosh, and Olympian Chris Giuliano, a transfer from Notre Dame. Tennessee’s team, in black jackets and swim trunks, walks out with a relaxed demeanor, one holding a towel, while commentators state they will be in lane four, featuring Ghi Caribe on the third leg and Jordan Crooks on the fourth. Florida’s team, in black hoodies with Gator logos, makes a bold entrance with hand gestures and head movements, with commentators emphasizing the significant presence of Josh Liendo, noting his absence from their SEC relay but his inclusion here. Finally, Arizona State’s team, dressed in white tracksuits, raises their arms in unison as they step out. The entire scene is lively, with event staff, ESPN camera crews, and seated officials visible along the poolside, underscoring the competitive and theatrical atmosphere of the NCAA championship.

The video opens with the Arizona State Team, dressed in white tracksuits and hoodies, emerging energetically from a smoke-filled, branded tunnel displaying names like Vergnes, Dobrzanski, and Kulw, as commentators highlight swimmer Ilya Kharun's presence on the third leg. They raise their arms in unison, showcasing unity, before the scene shifts to the California Team, confidently walking past a large 'CALIFORNIA' banner featuring names like Seeliger and Alexy. The commentators note that Cal will be in lane seven with Jack Alexy on the anchor leg, mentioning three returning swimmers from last year's fourth-place finish. Next, the NC State Team enters through their tunnel, wearing white tracksuits with red accents and swim caps, one with a red beanie, displaying focused composure, with commentators confirming their position in lane eight. As the teams move toward the pool deck, swimmers begin preparing for the race, now in swim caps and goggles, adjusting gear and stretching on starting blocks within a large indoor aquatic center adorned with '2025 NCAA SWIMMING & DIVING' banners. Event Officials observe from the poolside, and the ESPN Camera Crew captures the action. Commentators continue to discuss the race's third leg, mentioning Hubert Kosh, Kabe, and Josh Liendo, and referencing past performances and relay details, including Tennessee and Jordan Crooks anchoring, building anticipation for the "SEC matchup" unfolding in the pool.

event_blocks.summary: Shirtless swimmers, clad in caps and goggles, occupied starting blocks for the 200Y Medley Relay, meticulously adjusting gear and stretching. The camera panned back to reveal eight lanes, each designated with a team: Florida St in lane 1, Indiana in lane 2, Texas in lane 3, Tennessee in lane 4, Florida in lane 5, Arizona St in lane 6, California (referred to as 'Cal') in lane 7, and NC State in lane 8. Amidst bright arena lighting and the visible ESPN logo, two announcers provided commentary, identifying Texas, Tennessee, and Florida as potential top contenders. They also highlighted Indiana's possibility of breaking the American record despite their fourth-place seeding and Arizona State's anticipated strength in later legs. Event officials observed from the poolside. An official's command, 'Take your marks,' preceded a distinct starting beep, prompting the swimmers to dive into the pool simultaneously. As the race commenced, the camera tracked the swimmers executing the butterfly stroke, with NC State establishing an early lead. The on-screen timer displayed progress at 9.89, 13.5, and 20.24 seconds, while the leaderboard updated to reflect NC State's leading position. Announcers enthusiastically narrated the action, calling out a 9.89 split from McCarty of North Carolina State and questioning if the fastest backstroke split in history, 20.07, could be surpassed, noting the time to beat was 1:22.01, all against the backdrop of audible crowd noise.

NC State Team.description: Clad in white tracksuits with red accents and swim caps; one swimmer wears a red beanie, and they display focused, composed body language as they exit their branded tunnel, with their swimmer McCarty being highlighted by the announcer for a 9.89 split and taking an early lead.

Texas Team.description: Clad in white tracksuits with black accents; one swimmer raises a blue water bottle in celebration as they walk out, identified by the announcer as a potential top-three contender.

Florida Team.description: In black hoodies with Gator logos and swim caps; they make hand gestures and head movements as they emerge, identified by the announcer as a potential top-three contender.

Event Officials.description: Seated along the poolside in light-colored shirts, observing the teams as they enter and prepare for the race.

Arizona State Team.description: Dressed in white tracksuits with hoodies; they raise their arms in unison, showing unity and excitement as they emerge from a smoke-filled tunnel with their names and logo displayed, with the announcer highlighting their strength in the last two legs.

ESPN Camera Crew.description: Visible in black shirts with 'ESPN' logos, operating cameras and capturing the team entrances and poolside preparations.

Indiana Team.description: Dressed in black hoodies and red-and-white striped pants; they pump fists and display energetic, confident body language, with the announcer noting their potential to break the American record despite being fourth.

On-Screen Timer and Leaderboard.description: Digital overlay displaying race time, event name ('200Y Medley Relay'), and current leader ('LEADER: 8, NC State'), updating in real-time during the race, with the announcer referencing specific times like 9.89 and 20.07, and the target time of 1:22.01.

Florida State Team.description: Wearing black swimwear and hoodies, with swim caps and goggles; they walk out confidently, some with hands raised or gesturing.

California Team.description: Wearing black hoodies and swim trunks; one swimmer is shirtless with a towel, and they walk confidently past a large 'CALIFORNIA' banner with names like Seeliger, Okadome, Rose, and Alexy, referred to as 'Cal' by the announcer.

Tennessee Team.description: Wearing black jackets and swim trunks; one holds a white towel, and they walk with a composed, focused demeanor, identified by the announcer as a potential top-three contender.

Poolside Announcer.description: Standing near the starting blocks, wearing a white shirt and dark pants, holding a microphone and speaking to the crowd or team members, providing enthusiastic commentary on team prospects, race progress, and individual swimmer performance, including McCarty of NC State, and referencing specific times and records.

Swimmers on Starting Blocks.description: Shirtless male swimmers in swim caps and goggles, standing on numbered starting blocks, adjusting gear and stretching in preparation for the race; they respond to the 'Take your marks' command before diving into the pool.

raw_segments: The video opens with a close-up of shirtless swimmers in swim caps and goggles on starting blocks, adjusting gear and stretching for the 200Y Medley Relay. The camera pulls back to reveal eight lanes marked with team names and logos: Florida St (lane 1), Indiana (lane 2), Texas (lane 3), Tennessee (lane 4), Florida (lane 5), Arizona St (lane 6), California (lane 7), and NC State (lane 8). Two announcers provide enthusiastic commentary, identifying Texas, Tennessee, and Florida as potential top three contenders, while noting Indiana's potential to break the American record despite being fourth, and Arizona State's anticipated strength in the later legs, also referring to California as 'Cal.' The atmosphere is intense under bright arena lighting, with officials observing from the poolside and the ESPN logo visible. An official's voice commands, 'Take your marks,' followed by a distinct starting beep as the swimmers dive into the pool in unison, initiating the race. As the camera follows the swimmers executing the butterfly stroke, NC State takes an early lead. The on-screen timer tracks the race progress, displaying times like 9.89, 13.5, and 20.24 seconds, while the leaderboard updates to show NC State in the lead. The announcers excitedly call out the action, highlighting a 9.89 split from McCarty of North Carolina State, questioning if anyone can break the fastest backstroke split in history at 20.07, and noting the time to beat is 1:22.01, with the crowd audible in the background.

event_blocks.summary: The 200Y Medley Relay at the NCAA Swimming & Diving Championships featured intense competition, with Florida initially appearing to win with a record-setting time. The race saw powerful freestyle strokes from swimmers representing teams like Florida (in black hoodies with Gator logos), Tennessee (in black jackets), and Texas (in white tracksuits). The on-screen timer and leaderboard dynamically updated, showing Florida in the lead. However, a disqualification for Florida due to an illegal exchange from butterfly to freestyle dramatically altered the results. The Race Announcer and Color Commentator discussed the potential disqualifications, with the latter expressing disbelief and noting the loss of 40 points for Florida and the erasure of their record-setting time. Ultimately, Texas won with a new NCAA Record of 1:20.28, followed by Tennessee and California, as confirmed by the on-screen timer and leaderboard. The Florida team, initially celebrating in white and blue 'Gators' jerseys, faced disappointment, while Texas secured the victory. The event was marked by high-stakes competition, electronic timing systems for precise starts, and the watchful eyes of event officials and the ESPN Camera Crew amidst cheering spectators and NCAA banners.

Texas Team.description: Clad in white tracksuits with black accents; their swimmer is seen in the pool, completing the freestyle leg and finishing third in the relay.

Florida Team.description: Initially seen celebrating joyously in white and blue 'Gators' hockey-style jerseys with orange trim, waving to the crowd and hugging teammates, but later revealed by the announcer to have been disqualified for an illegal exchange from butterfly to freestyle.

Event Officials.description: Seated at a long table along the poolside, wearing lanyards and casual attire (including a red shirt and gray polo), some using laptops, others writing notes, and one speaking into a microphone, managing race logistics and announcements, their coordination implied by the formal announcements heard.

Race Announcer.description: Standing near the poolside, wearing a white shirt and dark pants, speaking into a microphone and providing live commentary during the final moments of the race, distinct from the poolside announcer by position and timing of appearance.

Tennessee Team.description: Wearing black jackets and swim trunks; their swimmer is visible in the pool executing a powerful freestyle stroke, finishing second in the relay.

On-Screen Timer and Leaderboard.description: A digital overlay displaying the '2023 NCAA Division I Swimming & Diving Championships' and 'University of Texas at Austin,' which visually confirms the 200Y Medley Relay Summary Results with Texas in first place at 1:20.28 (XR), a result also verbally announced by the commentator.

Swimmers on Starting Blocks.description: Shirtless male swimmers in swim caps and goggles, actively racing in the pool, executing freestyle strokes with visible splashes and powerful movements across multiple lanes, accompanied by the distinct sounds of water splashing.

ESPN Camera Crew.description: Visible in black shirts with 'ESPN' logos, operating cameras and capturing the poolside action and race progress.

Florida Team Celebration.description: Swimmers in white and blue 'Gators' hockey-style jerseys with orange trim, waving to the crowd, hugging, and displaying joy after winning the NCAA Record in the 200Y Medley Relay, a victory enthusiastically confirmed and predicted by the Race Announcer.

Arena Staff.description: Individuals in dark uniforms moving along the pool deck, handling equipment and assisting with race logistics.

Poolside Announcer.description: Two male announcers, speaking clearly into microphones, providing live commentary; one expresses sympathy for Florida's potential setback and the other later delivers the official race results, dramatically announcing Florida's disqualification.

Florida State Team.description: Wearing black swimwear and hoodies, with swim caps and goggles; they walk out confidently, some with hands raised or gesturing.

Spectators.description: Crowd members in the stands, some wearing team colors, reacting to the race with audible cheers and murmurs, visible in the background during wide shots and the celebration.

Poolside Equipment Crew.description: Individuals in casual attire moving along the deck near the starting blocks, handling timing devices and equipment, assisting with race operations.

raw_segments: The intense final stretch of the 200Y Medley Relay at the 2023 NCAA Division I Swimming & Diving Championships unfolds under bright arena lighting, filled with the roar of the crowd and the energetic, fast-paced commentary of the race announcer. The camera pans across the pool, capturing swimmers executing powerful freestyle strokes. The announcer highlights key moments, mentioning "German Prez of Texas" who was initially second, then correcting to "Will Modlin" with a 20.2 split, and noting "Crooks of Texas" in third. He excitedly exclaims about "Julian Smith" achieving a "perfect start" with a 22.1 split. The "big battle on the fly" sees "Leando of Florida" taking the lead with an impressive "19 flat" split. The on-screen timer and leaderboard dynamically update, showing Florida leading at 1:20.05, followed closely by Tennessee at 1:20.50 and Texas at 1:20.28. The announcer identifies "Scott Buff" as the Florida swimmer maintaining the lead, noting that "Crooks in Tennessee" is the only team that can catch them. As the race culminates, the announcer's excitement peaks, declaring "it's Florida! Scott Buff comes in at 1:20!" The Florida team, in black hoodies with Gator logos, emerges victorious, celebrating as one swimmer raises an arm upon reaching the wall, while event officials and team members watch intently from the bustling poolside.

The segment begins with a brief, unrelated clip from 'World of Warships,' showing a naval battle with a warship firing in a snowy coastal environment, before transitioning to the high-energy 2025 NCAA Men's Swimming & Diving Championships. The focus shifts to the Men's 200Y Medley Relay, where swimmers, including those from Florida in black hoodies with Gator logos and swim caps, Tennessee in black jackets, and Texas in white tracksuits, are seen completing the freestyle leg with powerful strokes and turns. The on-screen timer and leaderboard dynamically update, showing Florida as the leader. As the race concludes, the Race Announcer, visible poolside in a white shirt, excitedly declares the final time of "05" (1:20.05), proclaiming it the "fastest in history" and that "all the records smashed," though noting it's "under review." The leaderboard confirms Florida in first, Tennessee in second, and Texas in third. The announcer continues, stating, "Just what we predicted, my friend. 1-2-3 SEC! I said Florida was going to win this, they did," crediting "Liendo" for the win. Following their NCAA Record-breaking victory, the Florida team celebrates enthusiastically, raising arms, hugging, and smiling broadly, with one swimmer lying on the pool deck, exhausted but triumphant. They then don white and blue 'Gators' hockey-style jerseys with orange trim, waving to the crowd and displaying immense joy. The bright and lively poolside environment is adorned with NCAA banners, spectators, and event officials, with the ESPN logo visible, indicating the broadcast.

The intense atmosphere of the NCAA Swimming & Diving Championships unfolds with a high-angle view of multiple swimmers executing powerful freestyle strokes and turns in a relay, churning the water with splashes as event officials observe from the poolside. The arena's full scale is revealed with 'NCAA' and 'SWIMMING & DIVING CHAMPIONSHIPS' banners, while the stands are packed with cheering spectators. A large digital scoreboard confirms the 'MEN 200Y MEDLEY RELAY' with Florida leading, as the race announcer's voice cuts through the crowd noise, discussing a potential official review regarding a swimmer's feet being 'still on the block' during a critical exchange, noting the difficulty in verifying from certain angles and the reliance on 'electronic necessities.' Teams like Florida State (in black swimwear), Texas (in white tracksuits), and Tennessee (in black jackets) are visible near the starting blocks or in the pool. The announcer then specifically details the Florida team's 'exchange from fly to free,' identifying Liendo coming in and Scott Buff going out, while also highlighting that 'six teams' finished under 1:21, a significant increase from 'three last year,' and mentioning that 'Indiana broke the American record.' The on-screen timer updates in real-time as swimmers complete their legs, culminating in Florida's victory, met with crowd reactions and their celebration in white and blue 'Gators' jerseys, emphasizing the competitive energy of the championships.

The NCAA Swimming & Diving Championships unfold with a wide-angle view of a large indoor pool, its lanes marked by blue and white dividers, as the stands fill with cheering spectators and event banners line the poolside. Shirtless male swimmers, wearing caps and goggles, prepare on starting blocks for the 200Y Medley Relay. As the race begins with a synchronized dive and immediate transition to the butterfly stroke, creating splashes, commentators discuss a previous American record broken by a team, with one exclaiming, "How about that!" and another adding, "Should have mentioned that earlier, shouldn't we have?" The camera follows the swimmers, some gaining a slight lead, while the on-screen timer and leaderboard dynamically display the event name and current leader. As swimmers approach the first turn, executing sharp turns, the commentators focus on a specific swimmer's start, with one saying, "Watch right there... his feet... I think his foot is still on the block. I don't think that's a DQ." The other commentator then explains the electronic sensor system on the blocks that measures the precise time a toe leaves the block and hands touch the pad, aiming for a "perfect start" between .1 and .2 seconds, noting Omega's cameras for this. The race continues with intense competition and rhythmic strokes, capturing powerful movements and the engaged reactions of the spectators, whose background cheering is audible. Swimmers complete their legs, some turning at the wall for relay handoffs, as the on-screen display updates standings. The race culminates with determination, the final moments captured as swimmers reach the finish line, and the on-screen display shows the final results, including an NCAA Record for Florida. The crowd reacts with excitement, and the swimmers celebrate their performance, showcasing the intensity and skill of the 200Y Medley Relay.

The video opens with a close-up of a swimmer executing a powerful freestyle stroke, creating a splash as they approach the wall, indicating the final leg of a relay race. The camera then pulls back to a wide-angle view of the 2023 NCAA Division I Swimming & Diving Championships at the University of Texas at Austin, revealing the pool with blue and white lane dividers and a bustling deck. Over this, two male commentators are heard discussing a previous event's electronic measurement, with one stating, "I didn't see it," and the other explaining, "there's a review because it doesn't have it on our monitor here." They debate the accuracy of the timing, noting, "most of the times it pops up .4, .2, it did not pop up." The arena features a 'CHAMPIONS WIN HERE' banner above sparsely populated spectator stands, with ESPN and NCAA logos visible. The commentators continue, mentioning "high-speed cameras" and the high stakes of a "national championship," speculating on a potential disqualification (DQ) for a swimmer, with one noting, "the longer they take, Ted, the more likely it is to be a DQ." They discuss how a DQ could mean "the fastest time in history is wiped off the map," though one expresses doubt. Arena staff and event officials move along the deck, managing equipment, as the camera pans across the pool, capturing digital signage. The scene transitions to a closer view of the starting blocks, where shirtless male swimmers in caps and goggles are being positioned. At this point, a distinct poolside announcer's voice is heard, confirming a disqualification: "the announcer just announced that Virginia DQ'd." The overall environment is professional and competitive, with a clear emphasis on the high-stakes nature of the NCAA Championships, as teams prepare for upcoming races.

The video opens with a wide-angle view of the NCAA Swimming & Diving Championships, showcasing a large indoor swimming arena with a multi-lane pool, starting blocks, and timing equipment. Banners like 'CHAMPIONS' and 'NCAA SWIMMOVE' adorn the venue, while spectators fill the stands, their presence marked by a continuous background hum of crowd noise. Event officials are seen at a long table, focused on laptops and notes, with one speaking into a microphone, coordinating the race. A formal male voice, identified as the Poolside Announcer, details race specifics, stating "Virginia Tech two lane four. Disqualification for early takeoff" and later mentioning "three lane five." The scene transitions to swimmers mid-race, executing powerful freestyle strokes, with a yellow circle highlighting a key competitor. A Color Commentator, a distinct male voice, expresses surprise with an emphatic "Oh! Florida's DQ'd!" as the disqualifications are announced. He elaborates on the significant impact, explaining, "Three disqualifications and the third one erases Florida's win and record setting time," and later exclaims, "Oh my gosh," lamenting the outcome and the "40 points that go to Texas." The commentator continues to express disbelief and difficulty in discerning details, addressing a colleague, "Look, look, Ted, it's it's really hard to see because you get the sense that it's toe, but it's hard to see because this," as coaches and support staff remain active on the pool deck, all under the watchful eye of ESPN and NCAA broadcast logos.

The 200Y Medley Relay race is in full swing at the NCAA Swimming & Diving Championships, with shirtless male swimmers powering through the water in freestyle, creating visible splashes. A male announcer comments on an official being in the way of a swimmer's foot, while the ESPN Camera Crew captures the action. The announcer then expresses profound heartbreak for Florida, emphasizing the significance of this first race for building momentum, especially after breaking a record. Initial crowd cheers erupt, likely coinciding with the Florida Team's visible celebration in their white and blue 'Gators' jerseys. However, a second announcer dramatically reveals that Florida has been disqualified for an illegal exchange from the butterfly to the freestyle. The scene then cuts to an underwater view, where the On-Screen Timer and Leaderboard confirms the final results: Texas wins with a new NCAA Record of 1:20.28, followed by Tennessee and California, as spectators in the stands react to the unexpected outcome.

event_blocks.summary: The event concluded with the Arizona State Team and the Tennessee Team celebrating their victories. Arena Staff and Poolside Equipment Crew were present, along with Spectators. The On-Screen Timer and Leaderboard displayed the final results. The Poolside Announcer provided commentary throughout the event.

global_entity_library.Race Announcer: Standing near the poolside, wearing a white shirt and dark pants, speaking into a microphone and providing live commentary during the final moments of the race, distinct from the poolside announcer by position and timing of appearance.
global_entity_library.Florida Team Celebration: Swimmers in white and blue 'Gators' hockey-style jerseys with orange trim, waving to the crowd, hugging, and displaying joy after winning the NCAA Record in the 200Y Medley Relay, though not visible in this segment.
global_entity_library.Arena Staff: Individuals in dark uniforms moving along the pool deck, handling equipment and assisting with race logistics.
global_entity_library.Spectators: Crowd members in the stands, some wearing team colors (notably orange and red), reacting to the race, visible in the background during wide shots.
global_entity_library.Poolside Equipment Crew: Individuals in casual attire moving along the deck near the starting blocks, handling timing devices and equipment, assisting with race operations.
